page: Vultr vbm-256c-3072gb-8-mi325x-gpu is a bare metal server configured for high-density compute and accelerator-driven workloads. The system is powered by AMD EPYC 9575F processors utilizing the x86_64 architecture. It features 128 physical cores and 256 vCPUs with a base clock speed of 3.3 GHz, providing dedicated, non-virtualized CPU resources.

The server is equipped with 3072.0 GB of system memory, yielding 12.0 GB of RAM per physical core. For acceleration, it integrates eight AMD MI325X GPUs built on the CDNA3 architecture, delivering a total of 2048 GB of high-bandwidth VRAM. Local storage consists of 28,608 GB of NVMe SSD capacity, offering high-speed read and write performance directly on the bare metal hardware.

Given the integration of eight enterprise-grade AMD GPUs, massive system memory, and extensive local NVMe storage, this bare metal configuration represents a high-density hardware profile. It is designed for resource-intensive workloads such as large-scale artificial intelligence training, LLM inference, complex data science jobs, and high-performance computing simulations that require massive parallel processing and substantial VRAM capacity.
description: Vultr vbm-256c-3072gb-8-mi325x-gpu is a high-density bare metal server designed for intensive parallel computing and artificial intelligence workloads. The system features an AMD EPYC 9575F processor with 128 physical cores and 256 dedicated vCPUs operating at 3.3 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. It is equipped with 3072.0 GB of system memory and 28,608 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. For hardware acceleration, the server bundles eight AMD MI325X GPUs based on the CDNA3 architecture, providing a total of 2048 GB of VRAM. This configuration is optimized for large-scale machine learning model training, LLM inference, and high-performance computing applications that require substantial memory bandwidth and massive parallel processing capabilities.

tagline: A high-density bare metal platform featuring eight AMD accelerators, massive system memory, and extensive NVMe storage for parallel computing workloads.
